tasks.title: File Your Initial SSDI Application
tasks.what: Submit your Social Security Disability Insurance application as soon as you become disabled.
tasks.why: SSDI has a 5-month waiting period before benefits begin. Your application date sets your protective filing date — delaying even weeks can mean lost retroactive benefits.
steps: Apply online at ssa.gov/apply or call SSA at 1-800-772-1213 to schedule an appointment at your local office.
Gather your Social Security number, birth certificate, W-2s or tax returns, and a complete list of your medical conditions and treatment providers.
Document your work history for the past 15 years, including job titles, duties, and physical/mental demands.
Complete the Adult Disability Report (SSA-3368) with detailed descriptions of how your conditions limit your ability to work.
List all medications, dosages, and prescribing doctors, along with all hospitalizations and surgeries.
Keep a copy of everything you submit and note the date and confirmation number for every SSA interaction.

terms.def: The date SSA records your intent to file, which determines how far back retroactive benefits are paid.
The earnings threshold ($1,690/month in 2026) above which SSA considers you capable of working.

tasks.contacts: Social Security Administration: 1-800-772-1213 • ssa.gov • Local SSA office: ssa.gov/locator
mistakes: Waiting until you are certain you cannot return to work — file immediately to protect your filing date.
Understating your limitations by describing your best days rather than typical or worst days.
Forgetting to report all medical conditions — SSA evaluates the combined effect of all impairments.
Failing to keep copies of submitted documents.

script.intro: When calling Social Security (1-800-772-1213) to start your disability application:
lines: "I'm calling to file an initial application for Social Security Disability Insurance. I became unable to work on [date] due to [brief description of conditions]. I'd like to set up my protective filing date today."

"I have my Social Security number, birth certificate, medical records, and work history ready. Can I complete the application over the phone, or do I need to schedule an in-person appointment at my local office?"
"Can you also tell me what the expected timeline is for a decision, and what happens if my initial application is denied?"
script.note: Describe your WORST days, not your best. SSA needs to understand what you can't do. Be specific: "I can't stand for more than 10 minutes" is better than "I have trouble standing." Ask for a confirmation number.

tasks.title: Gather Medical Evidence and Documentation
tasks.what: Collect and organize all medical records, test results, and provider statements that support your disability claim.
tasks.why: SSA denies approximately 67% of initial applications. The most common reason is insufficient medical evidence — thorough documentation dramatically improves approval odds.
steps: Request complete medical records from every treating physician, specialist, and hospital for the past 12–24 months.
Ask each provider for office visit notes, lab results, imaging reports, and any functional assessments.
Obtain a detailed letter from your primary care physician summarizing your diagnosis, treatment history, and functional limitations.
Request RFC (Residual Functional Capacity) questionnaires from your treating doctors.
Document a daily symptom diary noting pain levels, fatigue, cognitive difficulties, and medication side effects.
Organize all records chronologically and create a master index.

terms.def: An assessment of the most you can still do despite your impairments — used by SSA to determine if you can perform work.
Medical opinion from a doctor with an ongoing treatment relationship — given significant weight in SSA evaluations.

tasks.contacts: Your treating physicians and specialists • Hospital medical records departments • SSA Disability Determination Services: ssa.gov/disability
mistakes: Assuming SSA will gather your records — proactively submit complete records rather than relying on SSA.
Gaps in treatment that SSA interprets as evidence your condition is not severe.
Not obtaining RFC questionnaires, which are among the most persuasive evidence.
Providing incomplete or illegible records.

tasks.title: Understand the 5-Month Waiting Period
tasks.what: Learn how SSA's mandatory 5-month waiting period affects when benefits begin and plan finances accordingly.
tasks.why: No benefits are paid during the first 5 full months of disability. Total time before receiving a check can exceed a year when you include processing time.
steps: Identify your established onset date (EOD) — this starts the waiting period clock.
Count forward 5 full calendar months from your EOD: your first SSDI payment covers the 6th month.
Recognize that SSA processing time (3–6 months) is separate from the waiting period — plan for 12–18 months.
Review whether any retroactive benefits are owed — SSA can pay up to 12 months of back pay prior to your application date.
Note that SSI has no 5-month waiting period and may provide faster interim income if you meet asset/income limits.

terms.def: The date SSA determines your disability began, starting the 5-month waiting period.
Back payments for months prior to your application date (up to 12 months before filing, minus the waiting period).

tasks.contacts: SSA: 1-800-772-1213 • ssa.gov/myaccount to track application status
mistakes: Expecting benefits within weeks — plan for 12–18 months before stable income.
Confusing SSI and SSDI timelines — SSI has no waiting period.
Not requesting an earlier onset date, which can increase retroactive back pay.

tasks.title: Build an Emergency Budget on Reduced Income
tasks.what: Create a bare-bones budget covering essential expenses while waiting for benefits.
tasks.why: Without a plan, savings deplete rapidly. Spending down below $2,000 may qualify you for SSI, but needs to be strategic.
steps: List all monthly fixed expenses: rent/mortgage, utilities, insurance, minimum debt payments, prescriptions.
Identify which expenses are non-negotiable versus deferrable.
Contact each creditor and utility provider to request hardship deferment or reduced payment plans.
Apply for LIHEAP energy assistance at liheap.acf.hhs.gov or 1-866-674-6327.
Apply for SNAP at your state social services agency or benefits.gov.
Calculate your monthly survival budget and use it as your planning baseline.

terms.def: A temporary pause or reduction in loan payments, typically deferred to later with possible interest accrual.
Maximum countable resources allowed for SSI — $2,000 for individuals, $3,000 for couples.

tasks.contacts: LIHEAP: 1-866-674-6327 • SNAP: benefits.gov • 211 Helpline: dial 211 • NFCC: 1-800-388-2227
mistakes: Not contacting creditors proactively — they have more options before you miss a payment.
Ignoring utility shutoff notices — assistance programs have lead times.
Cashing out retirement accounts (10% penalty + income tax).

tasks.title: Apply for Interim Assistance Programs
tasks.what: Apply for SSI, Medicaid, SNAP, and other programs to bridge income while SSDI is pending.
tasks.why: SSDI applicants wait 3–24 months for a decision — interim programs provide cash, healthcare, and food assistance during this period.
steps: Apply for SSI at the same time as SSDI if your income and assets are low — SSI has no waiting period and pays up to $994/month.
Apply for Medicaid at your state agency or healthcare.gov.
Apply for SNAP online at your state's portal — a single person may qualify for up to $298/month.

Check eligibility for state disability programs — CA, HI, NJ, NY, and RI have mandatory SDI; many more states (CO, CT, MA, OR, WA, and others) have Paid Family & Medical Leave (PFML), which is different but may also provide wage replacement.
Contact 211 for local emergency assistance including food banks and rental assistance.
Re-evaluate eligibility for all programs every 6 months.

terms.def: Supplemental Security Income — needs-based cash assistance for disabled individuals with limited income and resources.
Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program — monthly electronic benefits for purchasing groceries.

tasks.contacts: SSI/SSA: 1-800-772-1213 • Medicaid: medicaid.gov • SNAP: fns.usda.gov/snap • 211 Helpline • BenefitsCheckUp: benefitscheckup.org
mistakes: Waiting until SSDI is approved to apply for other programs — apply simultaneously.
Not applying for SSI because you assume you earn too much.
Missing annual renewal deadlines for Medicaid and SNAP.

tasks.title: File Employer Disability Insurance Claims
tasks.what: File claims under your employer's short-term and long-term disability insurance policies.
tasks.why: Employer disability insurance can replace 50–70% of salary, bridging the gap before SSDI — but claims have strict filing deadlines.
steps: Contact HR on your first day unable to work to learn STD and LTD plan details and deadlines.
Obtain your Summary Plan Description (SPD) for both STD and LTD plans.
File STD immediately — most plans require notice within 30 days.
Understand your plan's elimination period (7–14 days for STD, 90–180 days for LTD).
When transitioning from STD to LTD, be aware the definition of disability often changes from "own occupation" to "any occupation."
Most LTD plans offset benefits by SSDI — your total income may not increase but the insurer gets reimbursed.

terms.def: Waiting period before employer disability benefits start — typically 7–14 days for STD and 90–180 days for LTD.
Federal law governing employer benefit plans, setting appeal rights and procedures for denied disability claims.

tasks.contacts: Your HR department • Plan insurer on your insurance card • DOL Employee Benefits: 1-866-444-3272
mistakes: Missing the claim filing deadline — plans often deny claims filed even days late.
Assuming LTD approval guarantees SSDI approval — they use different standards.
Accepting an LTD denial without appealing.

script.intro: When calling your HR department about disability leave and benefits:
lines: "Hi, this is [your name] in [department]. I need to discuss a medical situation. My doctor has advised that I'm unable to work starting [date]. I need to understand my options for FMLA leave and how to file a short-term disability claim."
"Can you send me the forms for both FMLA and short-term disability? What are the deadlines for filing each? I also want to understand what happens to my health insurance while I'm on leave."
"If my condition extends beyond the short-term disability period, what's the process for transitioning to long-term disability? And who is the insurance carrier I need to contact?"
script.note: Call HR as soon as you know you'll be out. Even if you're not sure how long, starting the process protects your rights. Ask for everything in writing and keep a log of every HR conversation.

tasks.title: Navigate the 29-Month Medicare Gap
tasks.what: Plan for the gap between SSDI approval and when Medicare coverage begins — approximately 29 months after disability onset.
tasks.why: Medicare begins 24 months after your first SSDI benefit payment, plus the 5-month waiting period. A single hospital stay during this gap can result in tens of thousands in uninsured debt.
steps: Know the timeline: Medicare begins 24 months after your first SSDI benefit payment month.
Apply for Medicaid immediately — this is the primary coverage option during the gap.
If you earn too much for Medicaid, explore ACA marketplace plans at healthcare.gov.
Track your 24-month Medicare countdown and set calendar reminders.
When Medicare begins, also apply for Part D (prescription drugs) and consider a Medigap policy.

terms.def: SSDI recipients must wait 24 months from their first benefit payment before Medicare begins — about 29 months from disability onset.
Individuals qualifying for both Medicare and Medicaid — Medicaid can cover Medicare premiums and copays.

tasks.contacts: Medicare: 1-800-633-4227 • medicare.gov • Medicaid: medicaid.gov • SHIP counselors: shiphelp.org
mistakes: Assuming Medicare starts when SSDI is approved — there is always an additional 24-month wait.
Not applying for Medicaid — many states have expanded eligibility under the ACA.
Missing the Medicare Part B enrollment window (10% lifetime premium penalty per year late).

tasks.title: Maintain Prescription Access During Coverage Gaps
tasks.what: Secure uninterrupted access to medications during insurance transitions using manufacturer programs and pharmacy resources.
tasks.why: Stopping medications can cause serious health deterioration and undermine your SSDI claim.
steps: Contact medication manufacturers' patient assistance programs (PAPs) — most offer free or reduced-cost medications.
Search NeedyMeds (needymeds.org) and RxAssist (rxassist.org) for assistance programs.
Use GoodRx or Cost Plus Drugs (costplusdrugs.com) for cash-price discounts.
Ask your physician about 90-day supplies and generic alternatives.
Apply for Extra Help (Low Income Subsidy) through SSA for Medicare Part D assistance once eligible.

terms.def: Manufacturer programs providing free or discounted medications to uninsured patients meeting income criteria.
SSA program helping Medicare beneficiaries with limited income pay Part D costs — saves an average of $5,300/year.

tasks.contacts: NeedyMeds: needymeds.org • RxAssist: rxassist.org • GoodRx: goodrx.com • Extra Help: 1-800-772-1213
mistakes: Not applying for PAPs because you think you earn too much — thresholds vary widely.
Rationing or skipping doses to extend supplies.
Waiting until you run out to seek assistance — PAP applications take 2–4 weeks.

tasks.title: Appeal an SSDI Denial Within 60 Days
tasks.what: If denied, file a timely appeal through SSA's four-level review process.
tasks.why: SSA denies 67% of initial applications, but 45–55% of applicants who appeal to an ALJ are ultimately approved — giving up means walking away from potentially $200,000+ in lifetime benefits.
steps: You have 60 days from the denial letter date plus 5 mail days to file an appeal.
File online at ssa.gov/appeal or at your local SSA office.
The four levels: (1) Reconsideration, (2) ALJ Hearing, (3) Appeals Council, (4) Federal Court.
Submit new medical evidence addressing the specific reasons cited in your denial.
If Reconsideration is denied, request an ALJ hearing within 60 days — this is where most claims are won.
Prepare testimony about your daily functional limitations for the hearing.

terms.def: First appeal level — a review by a different SSA examiner. About 85% are denied at this stage.
An SSA judge who conducts hearings on appeals — approval rates range from 45–55% nationally.

mistakes: Missing the 60-day deadline — you must start the entire process over.
Not submitting new medical evidence with your appeal.
Giving up after Reconsideration — the ALJ hearing is where most appeals are won.

tasks.title: Work with a Disability Attorney
tasks.what: Retain a disability attorney to represent you through appeals at no upfront cost.
tasks.why: Represented claimants are approved at significantly higher rates. Attorneys are paid only if you win — 25% of back pay, capped at $7,200.
steps: Contact a disability attorney as early as possible — ideally before your ALJ hearing.
Understand the fee: 25% of back pay or $7,200 (whichever is less), paid by SSA from retroactive benefits.
Interview 2–3 attorneys and ask about their ALJ approval rate and experience.
Find attorneys through NOSSCR (nosscr.org) or your state bar association.
Be fully honest about your medical and work history — surprises at hearing can derail your case.

terms.def: Attorney payment only if you win — 25% of back pay, capped at $7,200, paid directly by SSA.
A person authorized to represent you before SSA — can be an attorney or non-attorney advocate.

tasks.contacts: NOSSCR: 1-800-431-2804 • nosscr.org • State bar referral service • Legal Aid: lawhelp.org
mistakes: Hiring an attorney who charges upfront — legitimate disability attorneys work on contingency only.
Waiting until the hearing date to seek legal help.
Assuming your attorney handles everything without your involvement.

tasks.title: Request ADA Workplace Accommodations
tasks.what: Use the ADA to request modifications to your job that allow you to continue working.
tasks.why: Many people leave the workforce prematurely because they are unaware of their ADA rights. Accommodations can help you remain employed and avoid the SSDI process.
steps: Confirm ADA coverage: applies to employers with 15+ employees; state laws may cover smaller employers.
Submit a written accommodation request to HR stating you have a medical condition requiring adjustment.
You do not need to disclose your specific diagnosis — only that you need accommodation.
Use the Job Accommodation Network (JAN) at askjan.org or 1-800-526-7234 for free guidance.
Engage in the interactive process with your employer and document all communications.
If denied, file a charge with the EEOC (eeoc.gov or 1-800-669-4000) within 180–300 days.

terms.def: Any modification to a job or environment that allows a qualified person with a disability to perform essential functions.
The required dialogue between employer and employee to identify effective accommodations.

mistakes: Making only a verbal request — always follow up in writing.
Disclosing more medical information than necessary.
Waiting too long to file an EEOC charge if accommodation is denied.

tasks.title: SSDI Trial Work Period and Ticket to Work
tasks.what: Understand SSA programs that let you test returning to work without losing SSDI benefits.
tasks.why: Many SSDI recipients fear losing benefits if they try to work, but the Trial Work Period gives you 9 months to work while keeping full benefits.
steps: Trial Work Period (TWP): work for 9 months within a 60-month window while receiving full SSDI, regardless of earnings.
In 2026, any month you earn $1,210+ counts as a TWP service month.
After 9 TWP months, you enter a 36-month Extended Period of Eligibility.
Enroll in Ticket to Work at choosework.ssa.gov or 1-866-968-7842 for free employment support.
Report all earnings to SSA monthly — failure to report is considered fraud.
Consult a free WIPA (Work Incentives Planning and Assistance) benefits counselor before starting work.

terms.def: SSA program allowing 9 months of work within 5 years while receiving full SSDI benefits regardless of earnings.
Free SSA program providing employment services to SSDI/SSI recipients ages 18–64.

tasks.contacts: Ticket to Work: 1-866-968-7842 • choosework.ssa.gov • SSA Work Incentives: ssa.gov/work
mistakes: Not reporting earnings to SSA — unreported earnings are treated as fraud.
Assuming any work will end SSDI immediately.
Not consulting a WIPA counselor before starting work.
